map.subtitle: Unit 5: Securing Applications and Data   •   2 days   •   LO 5.5.A, 5.5.B
map.intro: Everything for this lesson lives in this folder. Here is how the three surfaces fit together.
folderItems: Slides — Teacher Edition, Days 1–2 (speaker notes on every slide: timing, cold-call prompts, misconception alerts)
Teacher Guide (pacing with QOTD warm-ups, misconceptions, differentiation, CED alignment, auto-derived quiz key)
Guided Notes — Days 1–2 (student fill-in + answer KEY)
Exercise 1: Secure Design Identifier and Exercise 2: Input Validation Advisor (student + answer KEY)
Discussion — Baked In, Not Bolted On (3-part: Opening Day 1, Mid-lesson Day 2, Closing Day 2)
Quiz (student + answer KEY — 7 items, canonical in the Day-1 master JSON)
map.surfacesHeading: How the three surfaces fit together
surfaces: Slides = the daily instruction you project and teach from (Day 1 secure by design and secure by default, Day 2 control characters and input verification, then the quiz block).
This folder = your private scaffolding — keys, pacing, and facilitation that are deliberately NOT posted to the site.

Website = student practice that paper cannot do: instant grading, per-answer feedback, and mastery tracking. The student versions of the exercises and quiz live online; the answer keys stay with you and the site never displays them.

Naming: the CED topic is 'Protecting Applications'; LO 5.5.A covers secure by design and secure by default, and LO 5.5.B covers control characters and input sanitization/validation. All refer to this lesson.

exercise1.title: Secure Design Identifier
exercise1.intro: You are a product security reviewer. Five product decisions just crossed your desk. For each one you will classify it as secure by design (the development-wide principle), secure by default (features on out of the box), or an input-verification gap (a control-character problem). Part A works the review log; Part B extends the same skills. Everything maps to CB Learning Objectives 5.5.A and 5.5.B.
exercise1.narrative: Your security operations review board logged five recent product decisions. Each row records the company, the decision they made, the family it touches, and the classification you must assign. Read the log row by row, then answer Part A. Use the three-step method: anchor your first instinct, find the defining detail (is security built into development, switched on at shipping, or being checked at the input boundary?), then name the CED concept.

rows: A camera maker treats security as a requirement from the first design meeting, calling it a design principle rather than a feature added at launch.
Security included in ALL phases of development, including design
A router ships from the factory with its firewall already on and encryption configured, so a customer who never opens settings is still protected.
Security features enabled out of the box
A web team adds a function that strips out single quotes, double quotes, and semicolons (or errors out) before a form submission reaches the database.
Verifying input at the boundary for control characters
A vendor quickly publishes security-related product news and patches so other organizations can protect themselves too.
Sharing security updates quickly to raise security for everyone
An app encases each typed search term in single quotes and semicolons to process it, but never checks what the user actually typed.
Control characters used, but input left unverified

exercise1.scenarioNote: Decisions adapted from the Lesson 5.5 worked-table and Stop-and-Think items; concept names and EKs follow the College Board AP Cybersecurity Course and Exam Description (Effective Fall 2026).
exercise1.predict: Before you answer Part A, write one sentence: of the five decisions, which one is the trickiest to classify, and what is the single detail that decides it?

partA.q: Decision 1 (camera maker) is labeled secure by design. Quote the defining detail that proves it, and explain why the CED insists this makes security a design 'principle' rather than just a technical feature.
partA.answer: The defining detail is that the company 'treats security as a requirement from the first design meeting' and includes it in ALL phases of product development, including the design phase itself — that is exactly EK 5.5.A.1's initiative. It is a principle, not a feature, because security shapes how the entire product is conceived from the start rather than being a single visible add-on (like an encryption box) bolted onto a finished product. Full credit requires naming the 'all phases / design phase' language, not just saying 'they care about security.'

partA.q: Decisions 2 (router firewall on) and 4 (vendor publishes updates fast) are both inside secure by design but illustrate DIFFERENT ideas. Name the concept for each, and explain the one detail that separates them.
partA.answer: Decision 2 is secure by default (EK 5.5.A.3): the firewall is already on and encryption configured out of the box, so a customer who never changes a setting is still protected. Decision 4 is the radical-transparency-and-accountability principle (EK 5.5.A.2): sharing security news and updates quickly increases security for everyone, not just that vendor. The separating detail: 2 is about the state the product SHIPS IN (features enabled), while 4 is about an ongoing BEHAVIOR the company practices (disclosing updates). Both live inside secure by design, but only 2 is secure by default.

partA.q: Decision 5 is an input-verification gap, not a secure-by-default win. Name the three CED control characters the app is using, and explain precisely how leaving the input unverified could let a user manipulate the application.
partA.answer: Per EK 5.5.B.1 the three control characters are the single quote ( ' ), the double quote ( " ), and the semicolon ( ; ) — the special characters the application uses to encase user input so it can process it. The gap: because the app encases input in those characters but never verifies what the user typed, a user can sneak one of those same characters into their input and break OUT of the box the app meant to keep their text inside, then manipulate how the program runs. Encasing input is normal; the failure is the missing verification step from EK 5.5.B.2.

partA.q: Decision 3 (the boundary function that strips control characters or errors out) protects against three attacks the CED names. List all three, and explain why ONE function can stop all of them at once.
partA.answer: The three attacks are SQL injection, XSS (cross-site scripting), and directory traversal (EK 5.5.B.2). One function stops all three because they share one root cause — input the application never checked. The verification function runs BEFORE the application acts on the input and either sanitizes (removes the malicious control characters every one of these attacks depends on) or rejects (errors out and demands new input), so none of the three crafted payloads ever reaches the system. Naming the attacks alone earns half credit; the 'common weakness = unverified input' explanation earns the rest.

exercise1.partBIntro: Answer in complete sentences using the CED vocabulary. These are the same items available on the site exercise.

partB.q: A teammate says 'we made our app secure by design — we added encryption right before launch.', explain why this misuses the term.
partB.answer: Adding encryption right before launch is a technical feature bolted on at the end, which is the opposite of secure by design. EK 5.5.A.1 defines secure by design as an initiative to include security in ALL phases of development, INCLUDING design, so that security is a design principle, not a late add-on. A single feature added at the end does not make the product secure by design; the practice has to be present from the first design decision forward.

partB.q: Why does the CED treat secure by default as living INSIDE secure by design rather than as a separate initiative? Give one concrete example.
partB.answer: EK 5.5.A.3 states secure by design INCLUDES the concept of secure by default — building security in (A.1) and turning it on by default (A.3) are two halves of the same idea, not separate programs. Designing a feature in does no good if it ships switched off, so secure by default guarantees those built-in protections are enabled when the box is opened. Example: a router that is both designed with a firewall (secure by design) AND ships with that firewall already on (secure by default).

exercise2.title: Input Validation Advisor
exercise2.subtitle: 3 inputs - Sanitize vs. Reject
exercise2.intro: You are an application security consultant. Three teams bring you an input field and ask how their verification function should handle it. For each, run the control-character check (which of the three control characters could appear and how), decide whether the function should SANITIZE or REJECT 5.5.B.2 mechanism, and name the application attack the choice defends against. Advanced students also name the enrichment sub-type. Vague 'just clean it' advice scores zero.
exercise2.standard: CB Standard (required): control-character check + sanitize-or-reject mechanism + the attack it defends against, for all 3 inputs. Enrichment (optional): name which of SQL injection, XSS, or directory traversal the input most directly maps to.
exercise2.methodHeading: Three-Step Method - use for every input
method: Identify which of the three control characters — single quote ( ' ), double quote ( " ), semicolon ( ; ) — could legitimately appear and which would be a red flag in THIS field. Only claim what the field's purpose shows.

Decide whether the verification function should sanitize (strip the malicious control characters and process the cleaned input) or reject (error out and force the user to provide different input). Justify which fits this field better.
State which application attack the choice most directly blocks — SQL injection, XSS, or directory traversal — and tie it to the unverified control character it depends on.
exercise2.enrichmentNote: Enrichment sub-types: SQL injection = a single quote breaks out of a database query to inject new SQL. XSS (cross-site scripting) = control characters close a tag and inject a script into a page. Directory traversal = crafted input escapes the intended folder to reach other files. All three abuse input the application never verified, which is why one verification function defends against all of them.

scenarios.text: A login form takes a username and builds a database query around it. A user types the username  admin' --  including a single quote. The team currently passes whatever is typed straight into the query.

key.signs: The single quote ( ' ) is the red-flag control character here (EK 5.5.B.1): a username has no legitimate need for it, but the app encases input in single quotes to build the query, so a typed quote can break out of the intended value.
key.mechanism: Use the EK 5.5.B.2 verification function. Sanitizing (removing the single quote) is reasonable, but for a credential field REJECT is the safer choice — error out and force a clean username — because silently stripping characters could still alter a legitimate name. Either way the function must run BEFORE the query is built.
key.subtype: SQL injection — the single quote breaks out of the database query to inject new SQL; verifying the input at the boundary is exactly what EK 5.5.B.2 says stops it.

scenarios.text: A public comment box on a blog lets visitors post text that is later displayed on the page for everyone. A visitor submits a comment containing double quotes and angle-bracket markup intended to run as a script when others view the page.

key.signs: Double quotes ( " ) and other control characters are the concern (EK 5.5.B.1): a normal comment may legitimately contain quotes, so you cannot simply ban them — the danger is control characters that could close a tag and inject markup that runs when the comment is displayed.
key.mechanism: Use the EK 5.5.B.2 verification function and SANITIZE here rather than reject, because legitimate comments often contain quotes and punctuation — stripping or neutralizing the potentially malicious characters lets the real comment through while removing what could manipulate the page. The function checks the input before it is stored and displayed.
key.subtype: XSS (cross-site scripting) — control characters close a tag and inject a script into the page other users load; EK 5.5.B.2 lists XSS as one of the attacks the verification function protects against.

scenarios.text: A file-download feature takes a filename from the user and opens that file from a documents folder on the server. A user enters a filename that includes path navigation and a semicolon, trying to reach files outside the intended folder.

key.signs: The semicolon ( ; ) and path-navigation characters are the red flags (EK 5.5.B.1): a plain filename should not need a semicolon or directory-climbing sequences, so their presence signals an attempt to manipulate how the application resolves the path.
key.mechanism: Use the EK 5.5.B.2 verification function. REJECT is the better fit — give an error and force the user to provide a valid filename — because a filename should match a tight expected pattern, so anything containing control characters or navigation should be refused outright rather than partially cleaned. Verify before the file is opened.
key.subtype: Directory traversal — crafted input escapes the intended folder to reach other files on the server; EK 5.5.B.2 names directory traversal as one of the attacks input verification defends against.

discussion.title: Baked In, Not Bolted On
discussion.minutes: ~25 min total - 3 parts - Opening (Day 1) + Mid-lesson (Day 2) + Closing (Day 2)
discussion.overview: Strong applications are not made secure by a single feature added at the end — they are designed secure from the first meeting, shipped secure out of the box, and they check every piece of input before acting on it. Across three short windows in this lesson, your group will debate why security has to be a design principle rather than a bolt-on, decide which secure-by-design idea is hardest for real companies to live up to, and reason about why one input-verification function can shut down whole classes of application attacks.

parts.name: Part 1: Opening Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~8 min, after the bell ringer
parts.intro: In your group of 3-4, discuss both questions (about 3 minutes each), then share your most interesting answer with the class. No written answers required - just discuss and listen.

items.text: Question 1. Team A 'adds some security' a week before launch; Team B treats security as a design requirement from the first planning meeting. Why is the product built with security as a design principle more likely to be secure than the one with a feature bolted on at the end?

Question 2. Whose job is it that a product is safe the moment it is unboxed — the company that ships it with protections already on, or the customer who is supposed to go turn them on? Defend your split with one concrete example (e.g., a router, a smart camera).
Our group's most interesting finding from Part 1 (write 1-2 sentences to share):

parts.name: Part 2: Mid-lesson Discussion
parts.when: Day 2, ~8 min, right after the control-characters debrief
parts.intro: In your group, complete both tasks below.

items.label: Task A - Why three characters matter
items.text: Applications encase user input in three control characters — the single quote, double quote, and semicolon — to process it. In one sentence your whole group agrees on, explain why those SAME characters become dangerous when a user can sneak them into their own input.

items.label: Task B - Sanitize or reject?
items.text: Debate for about 3 minutes: a verification function can either sanitize (strip the bad characters and process the rest) or reject (error out and demand new input). For a public comment box versus a login username field, which outcome fits each — and what is the realistic cost of choosing wrong? Record your group's choice and one reason for each field.

parts.name: Part 3: Closing Debrief
parts.when: Day 2, ~9 min, after the worked-table review
parts.intro: As a group, work the prompt below, then one member shares your verdict with the class.

items.label: The 'one function, three attacks' case
items.text: A team adds a single input-verification function and it suddenly blocks SQL injection, XSS, AND directory traversal. Design the explanation: why can ONE function stop three attacks that look so different, what is the common weakness all three share, and when a developer says 'our input is sanitized,' what should a reviewer conclude — and NOT conclude — about the rest of the application's security?
Our group's verdict (the common weakness + one-sentence justification):
